coredata.dc:title: The controllable synthesis of multi-color carbon quantum dots modified by polythiophene and their application in fluorescence detection of Au3+ and Hg2+
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Jianing
Zhang, Dongkui
Xia, Jiangbin
coredata.dc:description: Herein, hydrothermal method was used to prepare a series of multi-color polythiophene modified carbon quantum dots. Under UV excitation, fluorescence their maximum emission wavelengths appear at 612 nm, 570 nm, and 540 nm respectively. The prepared CD-BTH and CD-BN can have specific detection of Au3+ and Hg2+ through fluorescence quenching effect. The detection limits for Au3+ are 3 nM and 5.4 nM respectively, and for Hg2+ are 23 nM and 90 nM respectively. CD-KN detects Au3+ specifically through fluorescence resonance, with a detection limit of 33 nM. Under the interference of other metal ions, three types of polythiophene modified quantum carbon dots exhibit excellent selectivity for the responsive ions. Meanwhile, this article also elucidates the law that as the electron withdrawing ability of the side chains of polythiophene derivatives increasing, the fluorescence emission peaks of the prepared polythiophene modified carbon dots shifts red and the fluorescence quantum yield is higher.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Morphology and structural characterization of multi-color controllable polythiophene modified carbon quantum dots
Fluorescence response testing of three polythiophene modified carbon quantum dots to 18 kinds of metal ions
The mechanism of multicolor controllable polythiophene modified carbon quantum dots for Au3+ and Hg2+ sensing
Study on the fluorescence sensing behavior of three polythiophene modified carbon quantum dots for Au3+ and Hg2+
Multicolor controllable polythiophene modified carbon quantum dots applied for Hg2+ and Au3+ detection in actual lake water

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Fluorescence changes in carbon quantum dots modified with polythiophene; (b, c) Fluorescence quantum yield plots of three types of polythiophene modified carbon quantum dots; (d) Fluorescence schematic diagram of polythiophene modified carbon quantum dots.
TEM images of CD-BTH (a), CD-BN (c), CD-KN (e) at 50 nm size and their particle size distribution.

Changes in fluorescence spectra of ethanol solution of CD-BTH (a), CD-BN (b), and CD-KN (c) after adding 18 different kinds of 3 mmol.L-1 heavy metal ions and two dimensional column diagram of fluorescence intensity changes of ethanol solution of CD-BTH (d), CD-BN (e), and CD-KN (f) after adding 18 different kinds of 3 mmol.L-1 metal ions.
CD-BTH (a, b), CD-BN (c, d), CD-KN (e, f) and their UV and FT-IR after interaction with Au3+ and Hg2+.
Preparation of multicolor polythiophene modified carbon quantum dots by hydrothermal method.
Comparison with reported Au3+ and Hg2+ chemical sensors in literature.
Detection of Au3+ in actual water samples.
Detection of Hg2+ in actual water samples.

head.ce:title: The controllable synthesis of multi-color carbon quantum dots modified by polythiophene and their application in fluorescence detection of Au3+ and Hg2+

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Hubei Key Lab on Organic and Polymeric Optoelectronic Materials, College of Chemistry and Molecular Sciences, Wuhan University, Wuhan 430072, People’s Republic of China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hubei Key Lab on Organic and Polymeric Optoelectronic Materials
College of Chemistry and Molecular Sciences

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: By adjusting the electron donating ability of the side chains of polythiophene derivatives, controllable multicolored polythiophene modified carbon quantum dots with different fluorescence emission wavelengths can be synthesized. The prepared polythiophene modified carbon quantum dots have a specific response to Au3+and Hg2+. Therefore, they can be used to make fluorescent chemical sensors.

Herein, hydrothermal method was used to prepare a series of multi-color polythiophene modified carbon quantum dots. Under UV excitation, fluorescence their maximum emission wavelengths appear at 612 nm, 570 nm, and 540 nm respectively. The prepared CD-BTH and CD-BN can have specific detection of Au3+ and Hg2+ through fluorescence quenching effect. The detection limits for Au3+ are 3 nM and 5.4 nM respectively, and for Hg2+ are 23 nM and 90 nM respectively. CD-KN detects Au3+ specifically through fluorescence resonance, with a detection limit of 33 nM. Under the interference of other metal ions, three types of polythiophene modified quantum carbon dots exhibit excellent selectivity for the responsive ions. Meanwhile, this article also elucidates the law that as the electron withdrawing ability of the side chains of polythiophene derivatives increasing, the fluorescence emission peaks of the prepared polythiophene modified carbon dots shifts red and the fluorescence quantum yield is higher.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDS) are carbon materials with fluorescence properties, and their surfaces are rich in hydroxyl, carboxyl, amino and other functional groups. They are zero-dimensional carbon nano materials with significant fluorescence properties [1–3]. In 2004, the carbon quantum dots were first discovered and named by X.Y. Xu [4] et al. Carbon quantum dots have excellent properties such as water solubility, photobleaching resistance, chemical stability and biocompatibility. Based on these excellent characteristics, carbon dots have been widely used in fluorescence sensing [5–7], photocatalysis [8–10], and cell imaging [11–13]. In recent years, it has been proposed that N, S co-doping is an effective way to improve carbon dots fluorescence, and has been widely used in domestic production. For example, Guo et al. [14] used citric acid and L-cysteine as precursors to synthesize N,S-doped carbon dots with memory effect, which can effectively inhibit charge transfer during steel corrosion, with inhibition efficiency up to 90 %. Zhang et al. [15] synthesized N and S doped carbon points by hydrothermal method using dopamine hydrochloride and L-cysteine as precursors, showed high intensity quenching of Hg2+, and could detect Hg2+ in the range of 5.0–5.9 μM with the detection limit of 2.0 nM.

Among the synthesis methods of carbon quantum dots, ultrasonic method [16–18], electrochemical synthesis method [19–21], microwave method [22–24] and other mature technologies are available. However, the material standards required by these methods are very high, involving strong acids and bases and very complex processes, which limits the further application of these methods. Therefore, to prepare a large number of high-quality fluorescent materials, we choose to use cost-effective, easy to operate hydrothermal method. In recent years, the research of polymer-modified carbon quantum dots has made some progress.

It is noted that the research of polymer-modified carbon quantum dots has made some progress in recent years. Polymers have a wide range of applications in modifying carbon quantum dot materials. They can serve as precursors for the preparation of carbon quantum dots [25,26] and can also be used as surface modifiers to modify carbon quantum dots [27,28]. Polymer carbon quantum dot composite materials are also a major research hotspot [29–31]. For example, polyethylene glycol can increase fluorescence quantum yield and regulate fluorescence emission [32–34]. Helena [33] obtained fluorescent carbon dots using PEG-200 and mercaptosuccinic acid as raw materials by hydrothermal method. The maximum excitation and emission are at 320 nm and 430 nm, respectively. Lyn Marie [34] synthesized S, N-doped PEG derived carbon quantum dots for photoluminescence and investigated the mechanism of S, N-doped photoluminescence carbon dots. Polyethyleneimine can improve fluorescence quantum yield and regulate fluorescence emission in the visible light band [35,36]. Saloni [35] successfully synthesized positively charged fluorescent carbon quantum dots with a size less than 20 nm using PEI as the raw material through a bottom-up method. Furthermore, organosiloxane can increase fluorescence quantum yield, regulate fluorescence emission, and avoid cluster enhancement of water solubility [37,38].

Polythiophene has a wide range of applications in optoelectronic materials, especially in terms of adjusting spectra. Polythiophene has the function of regulating the wavelength of fluorescence emission in visible and near infrared bands. The Stokes shift and fluorescence properties of carbon dots can be greatly improved by introducing polythiophene into carbon dots [39,40]. The preparation of carbon dots can be divided into “bottom-up” and “top-down” methods, but the “top-down” method with polythiophene as the precursor is limited to some extent. However, there are few studies on polythiophene modified carbon dots. It is the reason that the polymer is difficult to cut and disperse to reach the ideal carbon dot size.

In order to solve this problem, our team adjusted the electron supply and absorption capacity of the side chain of polythiophene derivatives. We used a large polar solvent as a hydrothermal solvent system to prepare polythiophene modified carbon quantum dots. Meanwhile, the luminescence rules of these polythiophene modified carbon quantum dots were investigated as well.

Sodium carbonate, anhydrous sodium sulfate, sodium chloride, ferric chloride, methanol, ethanol, ethyl acetate, dichloromethane, dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) chloroform, ethylene glycol dimethyl ether 1-methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP), tetrahydrofuran (THF), N, N-dimethylamide (DMF) are all purchased from China National Pharmaceutical Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. 3-thienylboronic acid, tetratriphenylphosphine palladium, 4-bromocarbazole, 4-bromoaniline 4-bromo-2,1,3-benzothiadiazole was purchased from Aladdin Chemical Reagent Company. All chemicals are analytical grade and can be used directly without further purification. Deionized water with a conductivity of 18.2 M.Ω.cm−1, used for all experiments.

According to the synthesis route shown in the figure below, three polythiophene derivatives p-BTH, p-BN, and p-KN were synthesized through oxidative polymerization by substitutes of thiophene at position No.3. The initial raw materials were 4-bromo-2,1,3-benzothiadiazole， 1-bromo-4-nitrobenzene and 3-bromocarbazole. Detailed synthesis methods and data can be found in the supporting information.

Dissolve three types of polythiophene derivatives, p-BTH, p-BN, and p-KN, in DMF solution to obtain a saturated solution. Take 5 ml of DMF saturated solution and 5 ml of acetylacetone for each of the three polythiophene derivatives, dissolve in 20 ml of ethanol, sonicate for 5 min, mix thoroughly, and place the mixed solution in a high-pressure reactor. Heat up to 160 ℃ for 6 h. Cool to room temperature and use 0.22 μ M nylon mesh filtration, the obtained solution was dialyzed for 24 h to obtain multicolored polythiophene modified carbon quantum dots (see Scheme 1 ).

The fluorescence quantum yield of carbon quantum dots modified with polythiophene derivatives was determined using a 0.05 mol/L anthracene ethanol solution（QY = 31.00 %):
Yu =
Y s
×
F u
F s
×
A s
A u
×
η u
η s
2
Among them, Y represents the quantum yield, Fu and Fs represent the fluorescence emission intensity of polythiophene modified carbon dots and anthracene, and As and Au are the absorbance of these two materials at their excitation wavelengths respectively. η is the refractive index of the solution, ethanol is used as the solvent here, so η u = η s.

Record the fluorescence data of polythiophene modified carbon quantum dots and anthracene ethanol solution at 360 nm. It is necessary to dilute appropriately to ensure that the absorbance of both is less than 0.05. The fluorescence quantum yields of the three polythiophene modified carbon quantum dots CD-BTH, CD-BN, and CD-KN were measured to be 33.2 %, 28.3 %, and 14.7 %, respectively (see Fig. 1 ).

Using a 365 nm ultraviolet lamp to irradiate a fluorescent colorimetric dish containing CD-BTH, CD-BN, and CD-KN in sequence, they exhibit red, orange, and blue fluorescence respectively. The fluorescence emission wavelengths of the three are 612 nm, 570 nm, and 540 nm, respectively. The side chains of p-BTH are typical electron withdrawing groups, while the side chains of p-BN and p-KN are typical electron donating groups, The electron donating ability of the p-KN side chain amino carbazole group is stronger than that of the p-BN side chain aniline group. Therefore, as the electron withdrawing ability of the side chains gradually increases, the fluorescence redshift of the prepared polythiophene modified carbon quantum dots results in a higher fluorescence quantum yield. This is because carbon quantum dots are abstractly regarded as a spherical shell model, which is composed of a carbon core in the middle and a spherical shell with functional groups on the surface. Here, polythiophene modified carbon quantum dots have a carbon core formed by the coiling and agglomeration of polythiophene chains in space, while the functional groups of the side chains are located in the “shell” region. The shell is a graphene like structure with an isolated island model, and its luminescence mechanism mainly consists of two aspects. Firstly, the active N on the functional group replaces the position of C, and there are many structures of “pyrrole-N” and “pyridine-N” within the shell layer. The stronger the electron withdrawing ability of the side ring, the more the overall electron cloud flows from the nucleus to the “shell” layer. The electronegativity and activity of N are greater, making it more capable of replacing C and easier to form structures of “pyrrole-N” and “pyridine-N” [41,42]. It also has abundant crystal defects and electron holes; Secondly, at the boundary of the isolation island, there are numerous active “carbine” structures with strong electron withdrawing ability of the side rings, resulting in a higher density of shell electron clouds, which facilitates the formation of “carbine” structures. Therefore, the enhancement of the electron withdrawing ability of the side ring makes polythiophene modified carbon quantum dots have more abundant defects, electron holes, and “carbene” structures, which are more conducive to the generation of fluorescence in carbon quantum dots and easier to generate longwave light. These all cause the fluorescence redshift emitted by polythiophene modified quantum dots, resulting in an increase in fluorescence quantum yield.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and structural characterization of multi-color controllable polythiophene modified carbon quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 2 shows TEM images and the particle size distribution of multi-color controllable polythiophene modified carbon quantum dots.Through TEM, it can be easily observed polythiophene modified carbon quantum dots with sizes around 6 and 7 nm, and these small black dots display their spherical structures. Through the particle size distribution test, it can be observed that their diameters were distributed in the range of 4–9 nm, belonging to the relatively larger carbon quantum dots. The particle size from CD-KN to CD-BTH gradually increases, which is also one of the reasons for the fluorescence redshift of polythiophene modified carbon quantum dots. The stronger the electron withdrawing ability of the side chain, the lower the electron cloud density of the thiophene main chain, making it easier for aggregation to form positively charged carbon core, which is conducive to the growth of the core. Polythiophene regulates the fluorescence emission wavelength in the process of modifying carbon quantum dots by changing the size of the “core”.Fig. 3. .

Fig. S1 shows the infrared and UV visible spectra of multi-color controllable polythiophene modified carbon quantum dots. It can be seen that the three have a clear amino peak in the 3470 cm−1 wavenumber range –NH2 3100 cm−1, a clear hydroxyl peak − OH, and a significant carbonyl peak −C = O in the 1600 cm−1 wavenumber range. There is a significant carbon carbon double bond peak −C = C in the 1470 cm−1 wavenumber range. In CD-BTH and CD-BN, there is also a carbon nitrogen double bond peak −C = N in the 2410 cm−1 wavenumber range, indicating that the surface of multi-color controllable polythiophene modified carbon quantum dots is rich in active functional groups. It is these rich functional groups that exist on the surface of the “shell” model, causing carbon quantum dots to emit fluorescence. UV visible spectrum display, The absorption wavelengths of CD-BTH, CD-BN, and CD-KN are 405 nm, 337 nm, and 418 nm, respectively, CD-BTH still exhibits double absorption at 508 nm. The above characterization indicates the successful synthesis of multi-color controllable polythiophene modified carbon quantum dots CD-BTH, CD-BN and CD-KN.

Table S2 lists the multi color controllable polythiophene modified carbon quantum dots. The element content of them can be found that both N and S elements were successfully doped, with a ratio close to 1:1. The introduction of S element has a regulatory and activation effect on N, and there is a synergistic interaction between them, which is conducive to the formation of “defect N” in the “spherical shell model”. Previous studies have also shown that the ideal value of N and S in N,S-codoped carbon dots is 1:1, indicating that multi-color controllable polythiophene carbon quantum dots are synthesized under relatively ideal ratio conditions and are prone to producing ideal fluorescence [43,44].

In addition, the XPS results revealed the bonding forms of elements in multi-color controllable polythiophene carbon quantum dots. As shown in Fig.S2 in surporting information, in multicolored controllable polythiophene modified carbon quantum dots, The N element mainly combines in the form of carbon nitrogen single bonds at 398.3 eV, 399.6 eV, and 400.5 eV, carbon nitrogen double bonds, and nitrogen hydrogen bonds of amino groups, which corresponds to the form of “shell” defect N in the “spherical shell model”. However, different chemical environments can also cause their peaks to shift left and right, indicating that CD-BTH The main reason for the luminescence of CD-BN and CD-KN is indeed the presence of these “active N”. Moreover, it can be observed that the proportion of carbon nitrogen double bonds in CD-BN is very small, which also explains why the characteristic peak of carbon nitrogen double bonds is almost not observed in the infrared spectrum, and carbon nitrogen double bonds are not the main binding form in this carbon point. Among the three types of carbon quantum dots, the different binding forms of nitrogen also lead to significant differences in the fluorescence characteristics of the three. In the same type of carbon quantum dots, due to external environmental interference, the bonding form of nitrigen in the carbon quantum dots changes, which will cause a change in their fluorescence phenomenon. This also lays the foundation for the sensing of heavy metal ions by carbon quantum dots below. It is interesting that the bonding forms of oxygen in the three carbon quantum dots are almost identical, all of which are carbonyl or hydroxyl groups, indicating that carbonyl and hydroxyl groups are also important active functional groups in the three carbon quantum dots. The similarities and differences in the bonding of sulfur between CD-BN and CD-KN are also worth noting, mainly in the form of C-S-C, indicating that sulfur mainly exists in the “core” of the “spherical shell model”. It is formed by the coiling of polythiophene chains in space, but not with N,O bonding indicates that the thiophene ring has not been damaged and N has occurred replace O on the ring. The difference in bonding energy between the sulfur should be due to the different chemical environments in which their sulfur are located, mainly due to the influence of the shell on the nucleus.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response testing of three polythiophene modified carbon quantum dots to 18 kinds of metal ions
ce:section.ce:para: In order to study the changes in fluorescence intensity of CD-BTH, CD-BN, and CD-KN under the influence of heavy metal ions, heavy metal ion fluorescence response experiments were conducted here. Take 3 mL ethanol solution of CD-BTH, CD-BN, and CD-KN respectively, and then add the prepared Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Al3+, Ba2+, Zn2+, Cr3+, Co2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Sr2+, Pb2+, Mn2+, Cd2+, Hg2+, and Au3+ metal ion solutions which are all 3 mmol.L-1 dropwise to these solutions. It can be observed that after adding 16 metal ion solutions, including Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Al3+, Ba2+, Zn2+, Cr3+, Co2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Sr2+, Pb2+, Mn2+ and Cd2+, their fluorescence intensity remained almost unchanged. However, after adding two heavy metal ion solutions, Hg2+ and Au3+, the fluorescence intensity of CD-BTH and CD-BN both decreased simultaneously. What's even more peculiar is that only after adding Au3+. The fluorescence intensity of CD-KN was actually enhanced.

As shown in Fig.S3 in surporting information, three types of polythiophene modified carbon quantum dots are shown with fluorescence photos of these 18 kinds of metal ions. It can be observed that Hg2+and Au3+ have strong quenching effects on CD-BTH and CD-BN, while Au3+ has a significant fluorescence enhancement effect on CD-KN. The above results indicate that CD-BTH and CD-BN have excellent selective fluorescence quenching effects on Hg2+ and Au3+, while CD-KN has a strong selective fluorescence enhancement effect on Au3+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The mechanism of multicolor controllable polythiophene modified carbon quantum dots for Au3+ and Hg2+ sensing
ce:section.ce:para: This article characterized the multi-color controllable polythiophene modified carbon quantum dots before and after combining with heavy metal ions by UV visible spectroscopy and infrared spectroscopy, as shown in Fig. 4 . After adding heavy metal ion solutions of Au3+ and Hg2+, the multi-color controllable polythiophene modified carbon quantum dots CD-BTH The absorbance of CD-BN and CD-KN both showed varying degrees of enhancement. In infrared spectroscopy, after adding Hg2+ ions to CD-BTH, the amino peak in the 3400 cm−1 wavenumber range significantly disappeared, and a clear binding peak between Hg2+ ions and N appeared in the 840 cm−1 wavenumber range. However, after adding Au3+ ions, a sharp double vibration peak of Au3+- N binding appeared in the 1410 cm−1 and 840 cm−1 wavenumber ranges. In CD-BN, both showed a blue shift of the carbonyl peak in the 1530 cm−1 wavenumber range, a new carbon nitrogen double bond peak in the 2480 cm−1 wavenumber range, and a new Ma+- N binding peak in the 800 cm−1 wavenumber range. Specifically, after adding Au3+ ions, like CD-BTH, a vibration peak of Au3+- N binding appeared in the 1410 cm−1 wavenumber range, while the amino peak and carbon nitrogen single bond peak disappeared after adding Hg2+ ions. After adding Au3+ ions to CD-KN, the carbonyl peak in the wavelength range of 1540 cm−1 shifted blue, The C-N single bond peak shifts red, forming a coordination double peak in the wave number range of 1410 cm−1 and 850 cm−1. The above results indicate that the main attack sites of Hg2+ are the active amino N on the surface of carbon dots and the defect N in the shell layer of carbon dots that exists as carbon nitrogen single bonds, and tend to transform them into carbon nitrogen double bonds or coordinated N forms; Au3+ tends to form complex and distinct coordinated N structures, resulting in coordination bimodal peaks. The change in the active N structure within the carbon dot structure leads to a redistribution of charge between the spherical and shell layers of carbon quantum dots, resulting in intramolecular charge transfer and fluorescence quenching in CD-BTH and CD-BN, while fluorescence enhancement occurs in CD-KN.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study on the fluorescence sensing behavior of three polythiophene modified carbon quantum dots for Au3+ and Hg2+
ce:section.ce:para: In order to further evaluate the detection ability of CD-BTH, CD-BN, and CD-KN for trace amounts of Au3+ and Hg2+, we conducted gradient fluorescence titration experiments based on these carbon dots. As shown in Fig.S4, when the concentration of Au3+ increases from 0 to 3.9 μM, the fluorescence intensity of CD-BTH decreases by 99 % at 612 nm, ranging from 0-3.6 μM. The interval exhibits good detection linearity, with a fitting degree R2 of 0.9999. According to the formula LOD = 3 σ/ S (LOD: detection limit, σ:standard deviation, S:linear slope), detection limit calculated as 3 nM. When the concentration of Hg2+ increases from 0 to 30 μM, the fluorescence intensity of CD-BTH decreased by 89.6 % in the range of 3–21 μM. The interval exhibits good fitting linearity, with a detection limit of 23 nM. In the CD-BN system, when the concentration of Au3+ increases from 0 to 1.8 μM, the fluorescence intensity of CD-BN decreased by 95 % at 570 nm. The interval exhibits good detection linearity, with a fitting degree of 0.9999 and a LOD as low as 5.4 nM. When the concentration of Hg2+ added to the system increases from 0 to 30 μM, its fluorescence intensity decreased by 97 %, ranging from 0 to 24 μM exhibits good linearity, with an LOD of 90 nM. It is obvious that CD-BTH has better sensitivity in detecting Au3+ and Hg2+ than CD-BN. As shown in Fig.S5, ion competition experiments were conducted respectively. The example competition experiment further proved that the detection of Au3+ and Hg2+ is not affected by the interference of the other 16 metal ions, demonstrating extremely strong anti-interference ability.

As shown in Fig.S6, when Au3+ solution is added dropwise to CD-KN, its fluorescence intensity shows an increasing trend, and the concentration of Au3+ in the system increases from 0 to 104 μM. The fluorescence intensity increased by 1260 %, ranging from 0 to 88 μM. The interval has good linearity, with a LOD of 33 nM. Although its sensitivity is not as high as CD-BTH and CD-BN, its selectivity and specificity are higher than both. Ion competition experiments and fluorescence stability tests indicate that CD-KN exhibits good selectivity towards Au3+ and can quickly interact with it to transmit fluorescence signals. Table 1 summarizes the reported Au3+ and Hg2+ ion sensors in the literature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Multicolor controllable polythiophene modified carbon quantum dots applied for Hg2+ and Au3+ detection in actual lake water
ce:section.ce:para:
Take the water sample from Donghu Lake, Wuchang District, Wuhan, filter to remove insoluble impurities, and evaluate the sensing ability of multi-color controllable polythiophene modified carbon quantum dots for Au3+ and Hg2+ in actual water samples through spiking method. The specific data are shown in Tables 2 and 3
, with the testing error of less than 5 %, indicating that the fluorescent chemical sensor developed by multi-color controllable polythiophene modified carbon quantum dots can be applied to the detection of Au3+ and Hg2+ in lake water samples.

Three types of polythiophene modified carbon dots, CD-BTH, CD-BN, and CD-KN, were successfully prepared using a one pot method. Their fluorescence excitation peaks were 612 nm, 570 nm, and 540 nm, respectively. As the side chain electron withdrawing ability of polythiophene derivatives increased, the fluorescence of the prepared quantum dots shifted red. When Hg2+and Au3+are present, the emission peak intensity of CD-BTH and CD-BN decreases significantly due to the internal filtration effect. And Au 3+ significantly enhances the emission peak intensity of CD-KN due to the fluorescence resonance effect. These carbon dots modified by polythiophene can be made of fluorenscent test paper further. However，only the influence of some metal ions on the sensor was tested in model sample solutions. The influence of other interferent molecules such as amines and other organic compounds on the sensor performance can be explored further. In any case, this work provides a new idea for heavy metal ion detection and opens a new chapter carbon dots modified by polymers.

Jianing Li: Writing – original draft, Formal analysis, Data curation. Dongkui Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Formal analysis. Jiangbin Xia: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful to the National Science and Technology Major Project (2023ZD0120802).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2024.124794.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Synthesis of Gd-doped CuInS2 quantum dots exhibiting photoluminescence and high longitudinal relaxivity

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Zheng, Y. Zou, J. Jiang, Synthesis of Gd-doped CuInS2 quantum dots exhibiting photoluminescence and high longitudinal relaxivity. Mater. Lett. 168 (2016) 86-89.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: High peroxidase-like activity of iron and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots and its application in immunosorbent assay

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W. Yang, T. Huang, M. Zhao, F. Luo, W. Weng, Q. Wei, Z. Lin, G. Chen, High peroxidase-like activity of iron and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots and its application in immunosorbent assay. Talanta 164 (2017) 1-6.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Mn(II)-coordinated Fluorescent Carbon Dots: Preparation and Discrimination of Organic Solvents

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Wang, T. Wang, X. Chen, Y. Xu, H. Li, Mn(II)-coordinated Fluorescent Carbon Dots: Preparation and Discrimination of Organic Solvents. Opt. Mater. 78 (2018) 118-125.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrophoretic analysis and purification of fluorescent single-walled carbon nanotube fragments
